sentences.text: 'Ku-risky.'
Good morning, Mrs.
Glickman.
Good morning to you.
Maria Glickman, G-L-I-C-K-M-A-N, M-E-R-A.
Kuricki, Q-- K-U-R-I-C-K-I.
1924, in Vilna, Poland.
What I remember?
The first that I remember is when my grandpa died.
I was three years and eight months old.
This was the first thing.
And then I remember when my sister was born.
She was born in 1932.
Her name was Asya Kuricki.
Nothing special, because we were too small to take us where.
What I remember is my father got sick.
He was 11 months in a hospital.
He came out.
And we lost everything in the bank.
And he was a painter, artist painter.
And you couldn't make a living.
So he went on to work with the theater.
He was an impresario.
He took small children, bigger children, and he made them for dancers, singers.
And he did go all of Poland.
To every city that he came, he had a theater, and so he made a living.
Not a bad one.
Szymon Kuricki.
I have to spell it?
No.
More about him.
He was a person who liked to help people.
He was-- in English, I don't know-- he was in the Jewish organizations, like [NON-ENGLISH], Beit Lehem, Hachnosas Orchim, Hachnosas Kallah.
For the children who didn't have parents, he was one of the people who helped them out by getting money from the rich people for the poor.

They're Jewish-- they were Jewish groups to-- Hachnosas Orchim, that means that somebody comes to the city, and he doesn't have where to sleep or what to eat, they put him in a place where he gets where to sleep and where to eat.
[NON-ENGLISH] was a place, at Friday and the whole Saturday, the whole families will come, five, six, eight people.
And they would get food Friday night, Saturday in the morning, Saturday in the middle of the day, and Saturday at night.
And Hachnosas Kallah, this was for helping young people to get married, to get them work, to get them where to live.
Then he was the vice president of a-- a hospital called-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not the Jewish-- I forgot the name.
Vilna.
We had two Jewish hospitals, the Jewish Hospital.
And the other one, I don't remember the name.
There were he was vice president.
So he helped out for the poor people, through the Kehillah.
Kehillah was the place where all the rich people sit together and think how to help the people who don't have nothing.
So my father belonged to them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother helped out, too.
When it was a concert, in the city, she would sit and sell the tickets.
That's all.
What was her name?
Sonia Kuricki.
When she was younger, she was the director of all homes for the children who don't have parents, after the First War.
And then she got married.
And she didn't work anymore.
She came from a very rich family. 99% of the family were doctors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all.
We had actors, painters, writers, poets.
I don't know.
We had everybody.
And everybody would come to see my father's pictures.
Not what he did for people, just what he did for people to sell.
He sold.
But not too-- very much, because Vilna was a very poor city.
We didn't have an industrial city.
We had a couple of factories.
That-- they had over 600,000 people, from them, 86,000 Jewish people.
I know we had a couple of leather factories.
We had boot factories.
We had glass, too, glass factories.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nothing else.
So the people had to live how they can.
So everybody did what they could.
It was a very hard life.
I can't say that we had a hard life, but I can't say that we were millionaires.
We weren't.
Because my mother's father lost everything before he died.
He had people working for him, 1,400 people.
He had-- we call it [NON-ENGLISH].
That means old stuff is-- they bought-- they bought it, and then they sold it to factories, like in Bialystok, in Poland, in Lodz.
In-- there were a couple of cities where they sold it.
But he was old and sick, and he lost everything, just the house and that.
That's all.
And my father's father went terribly, in a terrible death, in 1918.
Arath Kuricki Zalman got burned in his own house.
My grandma did go to the market with the lady.
And they came.
No, my-- not my grandpapa, not-- not the house, nothing.
When he-- he had burned alive when he was asleep.
By himself.
He-- he smoked a pipe.
Nobody touched him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I had a brother, Natan Kuricki, and a sister, Asya Kuricki.
My brother got killed by the Germans.
I don't know where.
I don't know when.
My sister and my mother runned, and they came to Siberia.
They were there up to '45.
I didn't know that they are alive up to '50-- '49.
I haven't seen my mother, my sister, up to '58, 17 1/2 years.
It's not nice, could be, to say.
I was the love of my father and mother.
Because when I was four and a half years old, my father took me to a ballet school.
The boss of the ballet school was a Russian lady, around from Petersburg, Sabina Dol'skaia.
And I was learning the ballet.
I finished in '39, two and a half months before the war.
I danced in the theater.
I sang in the theater in Jewish.
And they called me the wunderkind.
And everybody did know me, because of my pictures.
They were all over.
I was very happy.
But I didn't know-- this was June-- that in September 1, 19-- --20-- --39 will come out the war.
I loved school.
Not because I loved to learn.
I'm telling.
I loved the school because I was between children.
I loved very much children.
I did go to Esther Rubinstein School.
It was our minister, the Jewish minister, Rubinstein.
That was in his wife's name, this school.
It was a wonderful school.
We had to-- every morning, we had to sing the 'Hatikvah.' And that was just for girls not with boys together.
And I loved it.
What can I say?
I finished school in '39.
And the war break out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Russian came in.
The first three days was terrible.
They opened doors.
They cut the pillows.
They cut the blankets.
They beat up people.
They had the riot, the first three days.
And then it got quiet.
And for who it got good?
It was for the communist people, who were communists by the Russian-- by the Polish people.
So we lived a year and a half like this.
And then the Russian came back.
Describe the Jewish community before 1939.
The Jewish community?
You see, we have five Jewish communities, one in the middle of the city and three-- four in all four sides of the-- of the city.
And everybody spoke another Jewish language, had another intelligent.
And it was-- we didn't know for sure how the people from the other side live.
But what I know, through my father, is that most of the people were very poor but very intelligent.
We are the people who gave out the first university, Jewish university in the world.
We are the people who gave-- make from a jargon called, from a language that is taken from many languages.
And we make one language, with grammar, to learn and to know what you learn about.
In Jewish.
And this is the only university.
We have it now in the 92nd Street and Fifth Avenue and Jewish.
Everything was in Jewish.
And then they were in Hebrew and Polish.
We had 38 Jewish schools.
This is the only city in the world.
And because of this, we were called Jerusalem of Litha.
And that's-- and we had wonderful doctors, engineers, professors.
And they were wonderful, not just like doctors, like people.
They always helped the poor.
But because-- because there were many poor people.
And I, myself, helped my girlfriend.
[LAUGHS] She isn't here.
I ought not talk about it.
My mother teached me to always be good to people who don't have nothing.
Can't remember everything, but I think this is the most that I did.
When I-- when I got money for dancing, I didn't get the money.
My father get it.
So my father would give me 10 zlotas and-- and 50 grozy.
And I would take my poor girlfriend, and I would buy her food.
And I bought her a coat once.
[LAUGHS] I can't remember all of it anymore.
What was the relationship between the Jews in your city and the non-Jews?
I will tell you.
Me, I had friends not Jewish people.
I loved the Polish people.
And they loved me.
And they helped me in wartime.
If not them, I wouldn't be alive to go to the concentration camp.
And in wartime, they helped the Jewish people more, because the Lithuanian people, when they came to Vilna-- they got it from Stalin-- they said, we are the bosses.
And you will do what you want.
So because of this, I think, the Polish people understood and helped the Jewish people.
That's the--
Before the war, we had the student-- we had one university in Vilna.
And the students, they called them Endecja.
They would stay by the Jewish stores and not allow not Jewish people to go in, in a store, and buy by Jewish people.
No.
They did it by themselves.
Nobody told them not to, because Pilsudski was dead.
And his son-in-law was drunk, Marshal Rydz-Smigly.
He was married to Pilsudski's daughter.
And he didn't have time to look for his country.
He had time to drink vodka.
And this made very much trouble for the Polish people.
This was the problem in Vilna.
I can't say that somebody came and killed Jewish people or beat up Jewish people.
There was one fall in '39.
I did go from the movies with a friend of mine.
I was 14 1/2 years old.
And three students gave him a knife, here, in the right side.
No, my friend.
They didn't touch me.
I runned.
And I runned to my father.
And he came out with--
He's alive.
He is in Israel.
Yes, of course.
They were the Endecja.
The Endecja were anti-Semite.
They were-- they worked for like with Hitler, not together but for Hitler.
Endecja in Polish.
That means fascist students.
No.
No.
Never.
Never.
When the German came in.
It was in '41, July 22.
The war breaked out.
And they came, 23, early in the morning, Monday.
So three days, they came to look what we have.
And they did go away.
Then they came, and they took radios, like my father's pictures.
And we had a-- a child in our house.
And she was a child.
She said, I'm not going to say that, in the attic, we have every radio and everything.
So they went upstairs, and they brought down everything.
And six weeks later, we were in the ghetto.
Before the ghetto, I had to pay for electricity-- my father didn't go out-- me and my girlfriend, and they catched us.
They didn't touch us.
They just asked us to clean the offices, the places where they sleep.
And they let us go, twice.
That's all.
And then they took us to the ghetto.
Street by street, every street had police on horses, so nobody can run away.
They took people to the ghetto and to the jail.
My street and the second street of mine, did go to the ghetto, because it was straight.
Pilsudskiego.
This is the name of our general, who got the city back from-- from the Russian.
My father said that it will be war.
And Hitler will kill every Jew that he can.
But everybody would say, no, Kuricki, he's a special person, so he says so.
But it came like he said.
He was in the First War.
And he was two years in a camp by the Germans.
He spoke, perfectly, German.
And he did know what they thinked.
But before, the First War, the German soldiers were the best friends of the Jewish people.
You know why?
Because they couldn't speak to nobody, just to the Jewish people, because the German language is very, very easily understood by the Jewish people and the opposite.
So they spoke with the Jewish people.
And they were friends.
And they would help us.
And not me, I wasn't alive.
But they were good to them.
But after this, when the fang an, with the propaganda, that Hitler would come to, and he will be.
He wasn't a German.
He was an Austrian man.
And he lived in Austria.
But people think that he will give-- he said that there was 600,000 Jewish people in whole Germany.
And he said that all of them are rich.
And they have all the factories and all the money.
And the German people believed him.
So with this, he had to organize Gestapo, SD, Toten-battalion, and SS.
And he organized them.
And he came to Germany.
And they make the Kristallnacht.
They burned everything, the books, everything.
And then they fang an to threw them to the concentration camps.
Some run away.
It was all kind of doing of his.
No.
That-- my-- you see, my mother-- we runned everybody to Russia.
But the Russian didn't allow us to get through.
So the-- my sister, with my mother-- she was a little one-- my mother hold her by the hand.
And I don't know how they got through.
They got through.
And they catched them there.
And they sent them straight to Siberia.
And me?
We came back home.
We had to sleep somewhere.
We came back home.
And six weeks later, they took us to the ghetto.
And then we faengt an to work.
Yes.
Street by street-- one, two streets to the ghetto, two streets to the jail, Lukiszki, on a street [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came in the ghetto 52,000 people.
I didn't take nothing, because I was by my friend.
And my father was alone.
So what he did?
He took two luggages, you know, with the girdle, and that's all.
But something did get wrong, because one policeman couldn't stand that my father was, in 20 second, the prettiest man in Vilna.
Pince-nez is not glasses.
It's glasses with no frames and, here, a golden chain.
But he had three of them.
So two stayed there, and this was broken.
So he gave him a cup of beaten up.
And he walked in, in the ghetto.
And I didn't know where he is.
And he didn't know where I am.
Then we find each other.
And then we-- I had a girlfriend.
She was living in the same place where the ghetto was.
She is dead.
She died in the-- Nobody killed her.
She got tuberculosis, and she died.
So we had a place in her apartment.
This was two rooms and a kitchen.
We were 38 people.
Jewish people.
No, I didn't.
I did get to know them, when I was with them.
But before, no.
How could I?
It's a big city and there are 86,000 people.
You can't know everybody.
Nobody.
We came.
And we sit.
And nobody said a word.
Then, my girlfriend's sister was taken away and her mother-in-law.
And then we were-- we were the bosses.
And I stayed there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up to '42, February 15 or 14.
They catched me with five kilo potatoes.
That means nine and a half pounds potatoes.
And my girlfriend with five kilo of flowers.
And they took me to Gestapo.
And I was in Gestapo 11 days.
I bought them by the Polish people.
I sell a dress of mine.
And they gave me the potatoes.
We didn't have money, not one penny.
And then they send me, from the-- from the Gestapo, to the jail in Lukiszki.
And there I was from the end of-- of February up to the beginning of May.
I was between the 19 ladies, girls, and 19 men that officially were allowed, from the commandant of the SS, Weiss, to go out from the jail.
The first one.
All of the people who got took-- taken there, all of them got killed in Ponary.
Of the jail?
[LAUGHS] Every-- first of all, the Germans.
They said-- they looked like doctors.
Everybody wearing a-- in a weiss-- a white shirt.
And then it was the SS was around.
[POINTING] And inside, food would give us the people who sit themselves, but they were not Jewish.
And they helped us.
They selled us bread.
No.
In the jail.
In the jail.
Of course.
We had a jail in ghetto, too, but this was so big.
And this was a-- a-- a jail before the-- the war breaked out, I think 10, 15 years before the war.
So they-- they selled us the bread.
And they put it in the toilet where you put the papers.
[POINTING] But we took that, because we were hungry.
They gave us, twice a day, hot water, once a day, soup, , and 200 gram, big like this, bread.
And the days were long.
And we were hungry.
And they allowed us to walk, every day, 20 minutes outside.
Not outside the jail, outside the house.
OK.
